title: Northern Farmer (Old Style)
content:
Wheer ïŋ―asta beïŋ―n saw long and meïŋ― ligginïŋ― ïŋ―ere aloïŋ―n?
Noorse? thoort nowt oïŋ― a noorse: whoy, doctorïŋ―s abeïŋ―n anïŋ― agoïŋ―n:
Says that I moïŋ―nt ïŋ―a naw moor aïŋ―le: but I beïŋ―nt a fool:
Git ma my aïŋ―le, fur I beïŋ―nt a-gooinïŋ― to breïŋ―k my rule.

II.
Doctors, they knaws nowt, for a says whatïŋ―s nawways true:
Naw soort oïŋ― koind oïŋ― use to saïŋ―y the things that a do.
Iïŋ―ve ïŋ―ed my point oïŋ― aïŋ―le ivry noight sinïŋ― I beïŋ―n ïŋ―ere,
Anïŋ― Iïŋ―ve ïŋ―ed my quart ivry market-noight for foorty year.

III.
Parsonïŋ―s a beïŋ―n loikewoise, anïŋ― a sittinïŋ― ere oïŋ― my bed.
ïŋ―The amoightyïŋ―s a taïŋ―kin oïŋ― you to ïŋ―issïŋ―n, my friend,ïŋ― a said,
Anïŋ― a towd ma my sins, anïŋ―s toithe were due, anïŋ― I gied it in hond;
I done my duty by un, as I ïŋ―a done by the lond.

IV.
Larnïŋ―d a maïŋ― beïŋ―. I reckons I ïŋ―annot sa mooch to larn.
But a cast oop, thot a did, ïŋ―boot Bessy Marrisïŋ―s barn.
Thaw a knaws I hallus voïŋ―ted wiïŋ― Squoire anïŋ― choorch an staïŋ―te,
Anïŋ― iïŋ― the woost oïŋ― toimes I wur niver agin the raïŋ―te.

V.
Anïŋ― I hallus comed to ïŋ―s choorch afoor moy Sally wur deïŋ―d,
Anïŋ― ïŋ―eïŋ―rd ïŋ―um a bumminïŋ― awaïŋ―y loike a buzzard-clock ower my ïŋ―eïŋ―d,
Anïŋ― I niver knawïŋ―d whot a meanïŋ―d but I thowt a ïŋ―ad summut to saïŋ―y,
An I thowt a said whot a owt to ïŋ―a said anïŋ― I comed awaïŋ―y.

VI.
Bessy Marrisïŋ―s barn! tha knaws she laïŋ―id it to meïŋ―.
Mowt ïŋ―a beïŋ―n, mayhap, for she wur a bad un, sheïŋ―.
ïŋ―Siver, I kep un, I kep un, my lass, tha mun understond;
I done my duty by un as I ïŋ―a done boy the lond.

VII.
But Parson a comes anïŋ― a goïŋ―s, anïŋ― a says it easy anïŋ― freeïŋ―
ïŋ―The amoightyïŋ―s a taïŋ―kin oïŋ― you to ïŋ―issen, my friend,ïŋ― says ïŋ―eïŋ―.
I weïŋ―nt saïŋ―y men be loiars, thof summun said it in ïŋ―aïŋ―ste:
But a reïŋ―ds wonn sarmin a weeïŋ―k, anïŋ― I ïŋ―a stubbïŋ―d Thornaby waïŋ―ste.

VIII.
Dïŋ―ya moind the waïŋ―ste, my lass? naw, naw, tha was not born then;
Theer wur a boggle in it, I often ïŋ―eïŋ―rd ïŋ―um mysen;
Moïŋ―st loike a butter-bump, for I ïŋ―eïŋ―rd ïŋ―um about anïŋ― about,
But I stubbïŋ―d un oop wiïŋ― the lot, anïŋ― raïŋ―ved an rembled ïŋ―um out.

IX.
Keïŋ―perïŋ―s it wur; foïŋ― they fun ïŋ―um theer a laïŋ―id on ïŋ―is faïŋ―ce
Down iïŋ― the woild ïŋ―enemies afoor I coomïŋ―d to the plaïŋ―ce.
Noïŋ―ks or Thimblebyïŋ―toïŋ―ner ïŋ―ed shot un as deïŋ―d as a naïŋ―il.
Noïŋ―ks wur ïŋ―angïŋ―d for it oop at ïŋ―soizeïŋ―but git ma my aïŋ―le.

X.
Dubbut looïŋ―k at the waïŋ―ste: theer warnïŋ―t not feeïŋ―d for a cow:
Nowt at all but bracken anïŋ― fuzz, anïŋ― looïŋ―k at it nowïŋ―
Warnïŋ―t worth nowt a haïŋ―cre, anïŋ― now theerïŋ―s lots oïŋ― feeïŋ―d,
Fourscoor yows upon it anïŋ― some on it down iïŋ― seeïŋ―d.

XI.
Nobbut a bit on itïŋ―s left, anïŋ― I meïŋ―nïŋ―d to ïŋ―a stubbïŋ―d it at fall,
Done it ta-year I meïŋ―nïŋ―d, anïŋ― runnïŋ―d plow thruff it anïŋ― all,
If godamoighty anïŋ― parson ïŋ―ud nobbut let ma aloïŋ―n,
Meïŋ―, wiïŋ― haïŋ―te hoonderd haïŋ―cre oïŋ― Squoireïŋ―s, anïŋ― lond oïŋ― my oïŋ―n.

XII.
Do godamoighty knaw what aïŋ―s doing a-taïŋ―kinïŋ― oïŋ― meïŋ―?
I beïŋ―nt wonn as saws ïŋ―ere a beïŋ―n anïŋ― yonder a peïŋ―;
Anïŋ― Squoire ïŋ―ull be sa mad anïŋ― allïŋ―aïŋ― dear aïŋ― dear!
And I ïŋ―a managed for Squoire coom Michaelmas thutty year.

XIII.
A mowt ïŋ―a taïŋ―en owd Joïŋ―nes, as ïŋ―ant a ïŋ―aïŋ―poth oïŋ― sense,
Or a mowt aïŋ― taïŋ―en young Robinsïŋ―a niver mended a fence:
But godamoighty a moost taïŋ―ke meïŋ― anïŋ― taïŋ―ke ma now
Wi aïŋ―f the cows to cauve anïŋ― Thornaby hoïŋ―lms to plow!

XIV.
Loook ïŋ―ow quoloty smoiles when they sees ma a passinïŋ― by,
Says to thessïŋ―n naw doot ïŋ―what a mon a beïŋ― sewer-loy!ïŋ―
For they knaws what I beïŋ―n to Squoire sin fust a coomïŋ―d to the ïŋ―All;
I done moy duty by Squoire anïŋ― I done my duty boy all.

XV.
Squoireïŋ―s in Lunnon, anïŋ― summun I reckons ïŋ―ull ïŋ―a to wroite,
For whoïŋ―s to howd the lond ater meïŋ― thot muddles ma quoit;
Sartin-sewer I beïŋ―, thot a weïŋ―nt niver give it to Joïŋ―nes,
Naw, nor a moïŋ―nt to Robinsïŋ―a niver rembles the stoïŋ―ns.

XVI.
But summun ïŋ―ull come ater meïŋ― mayhap wiïŋ― ïŋ―is kittle oïŋ― steam
Huzzinïŋ― anïŋ― maïŋ―zinïŋ― the blessed feïŋ―lds wiïŋ― the Divilïŋ―s oaïŋ―n teïŋ―m.
Sin I mun doy I mun doy, thaw loife they says is sweet,
But sin I mun doy I mun doy, for I couldn abeïŋ―r to see it.

XVII.
What atta stanninïŋ― theer for, anïŋ― doesn bring ma the aïŋ―le?
Doctorïŋ―s a ïŋ―toïŋ―ttler, lass, an aïŋ―s hallus iïŋ― the owd taïŋ―le;
I weïŋ―nt break rules fur Doctor, a knaws naw moor nor a floy;
Git ma my aïŋ―le I tell tha, anïŋ― if I mun doy I mun doy.
meaning:
About: Alfred Tennyson, 1st Baron Tennyson (6 August 1809 ïŋ― 6 October 1892) was Poet Laureate of the United Kingdom and is one of the most popular English poets.

Much of his verse was based on classical mythological themes, although In Memoriam was written to commemorate his best friend Arthur Hallam, a fellow poet and classmate at Trinity College, Cambridge who was engaged to Tennyson's sister but died from a cerebral hemorrhage. One of Tennyson's most famous works is Idylls of the King (1885), a series of narrative poems based entirely on King Arthur and the Arthurian tales, as thematically suggested by Sir Thomas Malory's earlier tales on the legendary king. The work was dedicated to Prince Albert, the husband of Queen Victoria. During his career, Lord Tennyson attempted drama, but his plays enjoyed little success even in his lifetime.

Tennyson wrote a number of phrases that have become commonplaces of the English language, including: "nature, red in tooth and claw", "better to have loved and lost", "Theirs not to reason why, Theirs but to do and die", and "My strength is as the strength of ten, Because my heart is pure".

He is the second most frequently quoted writer in the English language, after Shakespeare.
